State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660              322-5901            322-6441            322-6444

March 1, 1985


Norman Ruestow
P.O. Box 897
Colfax, CA  95713

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-85-025

Dear Mr. Ruestow:

     We have received your letter requesting advice concerning
the Colfax City Council.  You have asked questions regarding the
application of the Brown Act to members of the City Council who
work in the same office, and regarding a City ordinance about
conflicts of interest.

     The jurisdiction of the Fair Political Practices Commission
is limitation and enforcement of the Political Reform Act of
Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections 81000-91015).
Your questions do not concern the Political Reform Act, and,
therefore, I am unable to provide the advice you have
requested.  I suggest you contact your City Attorney for advice
on these matters.

                                   Very truly yours,

                                   [signature]
                                   Kathryn E. Donovan
                                   Counsel
                                   Legal Division

KED:plh

February 4 1925

The Fair Political Practice Commission
Sacramento, California

The following letter is written, seeking informations
on legal opinions, as a concerned resident of
the City of Celix. I am also questioned by
many people within the city, regarding the
following questions. (I am also the elected
Mayor of the City of Celix, and have some uneasy
moments with this matter.) I am not writing
in an official capacity, as I must keep a good
relationship if possible.

Questions:
① How can three city council persons worked
together in a small parked facility in
Celix, avoiding the advantages of city business
abov from public scrutiny?

② Does this involve a conflict and possible
Brown Act violations (a quorum of council
persons)?

③ Is it a conflict due to marriage relationship,
one councilman as a son-in-law and
one councilman a father-in-law?

One councilmen has been elected, whereas two
have received recent appointments to the council
on split votes. Prior to the appointments the
Postal Service, the City Attorney and the individual
were advised and notified that the appointments
would continue to be questioned in this small
city, from people who seek fair play. Numerous

Qualified persons (most partial employees) presented proper applications for the Council openings but were rejected, without comment or interview.

Many immediate decisions are reached without discussion as soon as agenda item is presented in a public meeting, which gives one some concern.

Please advise at below address, my personal mailing address so that I may apply to those concerned people who have questioned me and requested by answer, hopefully in a plain envelope.

Respectfully
Yeomans Ruestow
Box 897
Colfax, Ca., 95713

Sec. 2-1.305. Conflicts of interest.

No Councilman or other official or employee, wh
shall engage in any business or transaction or have a
interest, direct or indirect, which is incompatible with
of his official duties in the public interests or wou
independence or judgment or action in the perfor
duties. "Personal", as distinguished from "financial",
an interest arising from blood or marriage relationsh
associations.

Specific conflicts of interest are enumerated

Reprint No.

Bozza appointed
to council seat

Frank Bozza has been appointed to the Colfax City Council, taking the place of his wife Marcia Bozza who resigned the position earlier this month.

The City Council voted 3-1 at the Jan. 22 meeting to appoint Mr. Bozza. Mayor Norman Huestow voted against the action.

In a special meeting Monday night the council passed a resolution on the appointment, and Mr. Bozza was sworn in by City Clerk Betty Delgado.

Mrs. Bozza had told the Record that the position was demanding a great deal of her time, and Mr. Bozza said his previous experience in city government will be an asset as he takes the position.

Mr. Bozza was the mayor of Colfax from 1978 to 1980, and he said he knows a lot of people in the area as well as each of the councilmembers.

Bozza, who is also a postal worker along with Councilmen Mike Talso and Richard Ballenger, said there is little danger of the city's business being discussed out of the scrutiny of the public since all three of the councilmen work in different departments.

He said he is usually out on his motor route while Ballenger is at the front counter and Talso is working in the mail processing portion of the city office.

Bozza said his familiarity with the area

Fair Political Practices Commission

Technical Assistance • •     Administration • •     Executive/Legal • •     Enforcement • •     Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662                    322-5660                  322-5901                  322-4441                         322-6444


February 11, 1985




Norman Ruestow
Box 897
Colfax, CA  95713

                                                          Re:  A-85-025

Dear Mr. Ruestow:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Kathryn Donovan, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        General Counsel

sections.question:
① How can three city council persons work together in a small postal facility in Colfax, avoiding the discussion of city business away from public scrutiny?
② Does this involve a conflict and possible Brown Act violation (a quorum of council persons)?
③ Is it a conflict due to marriage relationships, one councilman as a son-in-law and one councilman a father-in-law?

One councilman has been elected, whereas two have received recent appointments to the council on split votes. Prior to the appointments the Postal Service, the City Attorney and the individuals were advised and notified that the appointments would continue to be questioned in this small city, from people who seek fair play. Numerous

Qualified persons (not postal employees) presented proper applications for the council openings but were rejected, without comment or interview.

Many immediate decisions are reached without discussion as soon an agenda item is presented in a public meeting, which gives me some concern.

Please advise at below address, my personal mailing address so that I may reply to those concerned people who have questioned me and requested an answer, hopefully in a plain envelope.
